narrative: As an Asian male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including a solid LSAT score of 37—right above the typical student's 36.7—and an undergraduate GPA of 2.7, which is a bit below the average of 3.21. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of past students' academic records, family details, and bar exam outcomes, predicted a 68.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected; the school uses this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The three biggest factors driving this decision were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your LSAT performance, and your Year 1 school decile ranking, in that order of influence. Most critically, your low Year 3 decile of 1—far below the average of about 5.5—strongly pushed the prediction toward failure, as it signals weaker performance relative to peers at a key stage before law school. Your LSAT score, on the other hand, worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it shows strong testing ability compared to most applicants. Similarly, while your Year 1 decile of 1—much lower than the typical 5.7—also nudged the outlook toward failure, its impact was secondary to your later-year grades. Overall, your situation compares to typical students by having competitive test scores but grades that place you in the bottom decile early and late in school, which historically correlates with bar struggles for many in similar positions. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male from a supportive background—it's just the model's data-driven read on bar passage patterns. To move forward, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or additional undergrad courses, retake the LSAT if possible to solidify that strength, and consider part-time programs or gap-year prep that might reshape your profile for future applications. With intentional steps, you can shift these key factors and reapply stronger next cycle.
